comments.content: Talking with people I know who are into occultism, I’ve seen a weird pattern emerge in the past few months: a lot of people who claim to be into magic and the occult also claim to basically never read books and often lack anything approaching a full liberal arts education. This has struck me every time I’ve heard it as truly bizarre. I'm heavily informed by Dion Fortune, who writes in _The Training and Work of the Initiate_: "The good occult student should have a sound general knowledge of natural science, history, mathematics and philosophy," and goes on to explain that in order to successfully undertake esoteric studies one benefits from a good grounding in the exoteric. All of that said, I like to keep my snobbery closely in check and I value open-mindedness and flexibility. What’s your opinion on the degree of importance for aspiring occultists having or attaining a broad base of general knowledge?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's absolutely essential. My essay "A Magical Education" (in the book of the same name) goes into this in some detail. An ignorant mage is an ineffective mage; because the powers of magic are subtle, they need to be aimed skillfully at their targets, and a good general education is essential to provide you with the background that makes that skill possible. I specifically recommend that students of magic study mythology, philosophy, and at least one natural science, as a good basic framework for their work. As for snobbery, just don't parade around that requirement; smile and nod when you encounter ignorant mages, and save your advice for those who ask for it, and you'll do fine.

If magic is the art and science of causing changes in accordance with consciousness, then I suddenly see that it can be used to address quite a few things which I was missing until now. These questions unfold from this.

1) Can it be used to address an addiction? If so, what would you recommend? My addiction is a porn addiction, if that matters.

2) Do you have advice for dealing with the aftermath of growing up with parents who strongly expressed the devouring mother archetype? I'll be moving out in a little over a month, and intend to cut ties as much as possible; I'm doing some journaling, but I'm also hoping there's more I can do, since it's currently a very slow and painful process.

3) My parents are filthy rich and have used money as a way to control me; and one result is that they left me with a pathological fear of being poor. I'm quite sure that it will go away on its own once I'm living on my own and clearly able to get by on a fraction of what I currently subconsciously think I need, but do you have any advice on how to get over this fear?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, but not immediately. You need to build strength of will and self-knowledge before you can tackle a well-entrenched addiction. Concentrate on other things first.

2) The essential trick is to remember that your parents are not the archetype. They're just a couple of aging people with psychological hangups. The archetype, the thing that gives them power in your emotional life, is internal to you and you can confront it in yourself without having to mess with them.

3) The only cure I know of for that fear is to become poor and discover that it's not a problem. It's like losing a job -- everyone I know who was terrified about losing a job got over it once that happened and they found another one. "Plunge thee into the maelstrom of life!" That's Thor's advice, and it's worth taking. ;-)

You can usually tell when someone has come from a poor background, they are interesting people. They aren't just another identical person that comes out of a little box, or plastic people as Frank Zappa called them. They have had to face adverse times and have also figure out how to overcome them. In over coming issues of lacking they become stronger people. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No matter how little money you have, spend less than you make. If that means you need to live on rice and beans, learn to like them. (At this point they're one of my comfort foods.) comments.content: I've been practicing DMH for a little while now, and was hoping to learn more about the higher self, and bringing about an awakening. Hopefully this list isn't too obnoxious: Are there any books you can recommend on the subject? Are there any practices you can recommend for awakening/contacting the higher self? Is Druidry well suited to this sort of thing or is there another path that is more appropriate to such an aim?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'll take those in reverse order. (1) The style of Druidry taught in the DMH is as effective at doing that as any other system of magical training. (2) Daily meditation is the essential practice for this -- it's not fast but it does the job. (3) I don't know of any, and there's a reason for that. One of the very common problems in magical training is that people confuse their egos with their higher selves, and the more details about the higher self they get from books, the more likely their egos are to dress up in the mental equivalent of drag and prance around pretending to be their higher selves. It's much more useful simply to do the practices, concentrate on other goals, and let the opening of contact with the higher self unfold in a natural way, as it will.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This is excellent news. Daily meditation I can handle. I guess its a lingering trace of consumerism that I was hoping I could get it faster, since it seems over-ridingly important now. Thank you.

comments.content: Astrology question: given how lots of people make a big deal out of a lot of things and blow stuff out of proportion, how significant is the presence of a grand cross in a natal chart?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Depends on the natal chart. A grand cross where the four planets aren't especially strongly placed, essentially or accidentally, needn't mean a great deal. A grand cross where they're strong is another matter. The crucial thing to remember is that squares and oppositions (which is of course what a grand cross is made of) are sources of strength to the personality; a chart that's all trines and sextiles often results in one of those vague ineffectual personalities that drift through life in a warm blur. Look in the charts of people who accomplished great things in the world and you'll usually find squares and oppositions, and quite often a grand cross.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In my chart I have Mercury and Venus in Gemini, Mars in Virgo, Jupiter in Sagittarius, and Saturn in Pisces in a grand cross, with all but Saturn angular, and Saturn ruling the chart. Does this sound like it could become a source of intense strength?

How do you go about making hostile aspects into a source of strength?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nice. With Mercury in Gemini and Jupiter in Sagittarius you've got two of the planets in their rulerships, so strong and positive, and nobody in their detriment or fall. That could be a very strong chart. You'll need to work on controlling your enthusiasm with clear thinking and careful attention to the details, but that should be doable. More generally, you make hostile aspects into sources of strength by learning to balance the forces they represent, holding them in equilibrium in your life and going to one side or the other only when you choose to do so, to the degree that you choose to do so.

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Mutual reception is when two planets are in each other's rulership. They act as though they've swapped places and each is in its own rulership. So you've got the equivalent of Saturn and Neptune in their own signs, thus strong and balanced. Go do something spectacular with your life.

I really enjoyed your book, “A King in Orange.” Among other very helpful aspects, I found especially helpful the part on the tamanous spirit. Ever since accepting a basic polytheism and learning about the occult, I’ve felt pulled in multiple directions. It was disorienting at first but now I realize that it has been part of this tamanousian process of developing a very individualized personal spiritual relationship.

So, I want to offer a heartfelt thanks for your book! For many reasons, I got a lot out of it. It was very helpful in understanding the current political situation and how things might play out in the coming years. But perhaps the most lasting impact your book will have on me is how its given me an unexpected direction as I walk my spiritual path. Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)

Okay, this diagram is the structure of the human individual. A is the nous, the "simple breath of God" at the core of the self. B is the intellectus -- what we'd call the higher, intuitive mind -- and C is the mens, the thinking and reflective mind. D is the spiritus, what we'd call the astral body, and E is the anima, what we'd call the etheric body or body of life force. F is the corpus or material body.

If you've been enjoying these nickel tours, you might also appreciate Joscelyn Godwin's book, The Greater and Lesser Worlds of Robert Fludd (Inner Traditions, 2019). For those already familiar with Fludd, or who are comfortable enough with Fludd's Latin to get your own start, there's probably not a lot here, apart from exceptionally crisp reproductions of the plates. But for everyone else, it might be worth considering.

replies.title: (no subject)

"Do you know of some sort of old-fashioned manual or similar on how to become an adult?"

I don't know of any such book. Most of the real adults whom I know got there by never avoiding the hard work they had to do in order to meet whatever hard challenges--there are always hard challenges!--life happened to throw at them. It's OK to fear hard challenges, but not to run away from them or ignore them. Being an adult is a highly active and foresightful kind of life, not a passive one or a lazy one. A life without hard challenges keeps one from becoming an adult. You can get tired of challenges, and rest from the labor of meeting them for a while; but then it's always "back to the kitchen sink and wash up the dirty dishes"!

comments.title: OCD
comments.content: Are there any occult explanations and/or treatments for obsessive-compulsive disorder?   In particular, a compulsion to repeatedly check one's actions for accuracy and correctness.

replies.title: Re: OCD
replies.content: As far as I know it's psychological in nature, not occult, though it can involve psychological burdens from previous lives. Most often, though, it's a function of unresolved childhood anxieties and traumas.

replies.title: Re: OCD
replies.content:
The four occult pathways to enlightenment correspond to four fundamental energetic alignments in life. Everything everywhere, including ourselves, has an affinity for one of those pathways. Each of the four different paths tends to express balance and imbalance in its own unique ways. In humans, the four enlightenments reflect the four paths’ different ways of expressing a very high level of balance. Various pathologies reflect the four paths’ different ways of expressing a high level of imbalance. Obsessive-compulsive disorder is one of those pathologies.

The hero path, so deeply grounded in body and experience, tends to express itself along a spectrum that includes both heroism and compulsive craving. Those are both expressions of deep grounding, presence, and longing to make manifest in the physical plane. Their profound difference in how they affect our lives comes from whether their expression of the hero path is balanced or imbalanced. Oddly, obsessive-compulsive disorder is a good indicator that one is very much traveling along the hero path, although I know that it feels much more like stumbling in place.

Resolving the imbalances within ourselves is not the easiest of challenges, regardless of which path you find yourself to be on, but it is extraordinarily rewarding. Contemplating those imbalances, their causes, their protective roles, how they alter the rhythm of our lives and why, and their often amusing effects is the miraculous solvent that can unexpectedly resolve imbalance. Of course, approaching contemplation obsessively is unlikely to ever weaken obsession’s hold! Letting yourself feel and experience and imagine and even create the condition, in other words playing with it, has more of the real flavor of contemplation about it.

Whichever parents, teachers, siblings, karma etc. helped to plant and nurture the imbalances within you were giving you a mighty thrust block to play with. Who knows what direction exploring them could launch you in? Or, perhaps, if you yourself take up caring for and nurturing those discarded imbalances that were planted within you, they may one night grow up into a mighty beanstalk, leading you to treasures and adventures as yet unimagined.

Until we can learn to look directly in the eye of the thing we have been resisting and fighting against, appreciating every nuance and facet of it, all its strengths and weaknesses, we are unlikely to be freed of its controlling influence. Once we can truly befriend our nemesis, laughing with it at its unpredictable absurdity and weeping with it at its pain and grief, often we no longer want to be rid of it. Somehow, embracing all that imbalanced, destabilizing disorder we find within ourselves transforms it into an irreplaceable gift we had so-long been overlooking.

John Michael seems to dance along miraculously gracefully with his Asperger’s, despite its reputation for having two left feet. Be gentle with yourself and your "disorder" — you two are traveling together along this path for some reason. I wonder what that might be?

— Christophe

comments.title: Jesus, Mary and an ancient tradition
comments.content:
Hello!

replies.title: Re: Jesus, Mary and an ancient tradition
replies.content:
1) That theory has been in circulation in some alternative-culture circles for about a century and a half. The claim that he was the illegitimate son of a Roman soldier has been around a lot longer -- that's the story in the Toledot Yeshu, which was certainly around by the 9th century and may go back much further.

2) Those persistent rumors were manufactured in the very late 20th century by specific, readily identifiable people, who were riffing off Pierre Plantard's notorious "Prieure de Sion" hoax. (There's a nice summary of the hoax here.)

3) None that I'd consider reliable.

4) You'd have to ask someone with a background in Indian spirituality, which I don't have. The claim in some British occult circles is that there was such a tradition in very ancient times in a land area that drowned at the end of the last ice age, that survivors of the Sacred Clan (the group thus inbred) reached safety in Britain, and that's why the Celts have family lines in which "second sight" is common. Several attempts to do the same thing in modern times have taken place, without noticeable results; if that approach works, it probably takes centuries of careful breeding of precisely chosen bloodlines.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not JMG, but from a buddhist perspective: if you become aware of something but can't resolve it, one of three things is happening. First possibility is that it isn't the biggest problem but it is relevant to what you should be working on. This basically means the unresolvable karma is connected to something that is either hidden or you aren't able to bring up to yourself. You may wish to meditate on it, and possibly consider what other work could be done that is only related to it. Second possibility is that it is actually severe enough that it will "hang" until you are able to deal with it. This usually means the karma will be intense enough that it will be highly disruptive or isn't easy to deal with without outside help. Third possibility, and this is an exercise for your own judgement, is that a delusion has set in and the mind has fixed upon some imagined past sin to avoid doing real initiatic work. In the second case, you can deal with it whenever is feels appropriate. The easiest way to set it off is to simply put your attention on it and try to communicate with it. Basically, "I am ready to attend to this now." Magic would force the issue sooner rather than later, but it isn't necessary to deal with suspended karma.

Dear Mr Greer,


This is not so much a question as an update on omens and communications with higher powers.

I am currently in transit. I have a job in Japan, but I cannot get my work visa until the border re-opens. So I have been learning Japanese and waiting...

I pray regularly to the Lady Bast, who I have been close to for many years. More often than not, I see messages in the random sentences that appear, often with a dry sense of humour evident. Phrases such as " all in good time" when I have asked to get me to Japan.

Recently I began to despair and I applied for a job in Indonesia and started learning Indonesian as a backup plan. So I am waiting for the Indonesian company to reply and I go to the only large bookshop in town to look for a phrasebook. To my surprise they have not a single Indonesian phrasebook and a whole shelf of Japanese books, odd since the last time I went they had but one.

I thought it over and decided I really did not want the Indonesian job, I wanted to go to Japan more than ever.

Add to this, recently I have been seeing foxes all over the place in daytime. This is somewhat unusual, but then I have been praying to Amateratsu the chief Kami, Sun Goddess of Japan, in polite Japanese, and her messengers are foxes. So receiving the message I have withdrawn the Indonesian plan and rededicated myself to learning Japanese and trying to be patient...

Most of my greatest errors in life have happened because I disregarded the direction of forces in life and tried to go against the flow. Now I am at least trying to pay attention...

Many thanks for making this space available.

Best regards Ben from the north.

comments.title: The Artist’s Way

After making way through the intro and week 1, many of the exercises feel like something that could be included in OBOD’s bardic grade (which I am ever so slowly working my way through by keep getting derailed). replies.title: Re: The Artist’s Way
replies.content: I can't speak to it from an occult standpoint but I know people who think highly of it.

replies.title: Re: The Artist’s Way
replies.content:
Obviously not JMG, but I've worked my way through Artist's Way once, and have sporadically used something like Morning Pages since then. From what I recall of the book, it has a vaguely Christian/Jungian/Late 20th Century "Spiritual" vibe, but didn't seem to include anything that wasn't adequately explained as psychological in nature.

I think the book is most useful for folks who are either completely cut off from feeling creative, and/or are overly harsh with their self-judgment of their creativity. If you feel like you would benefit from giving yourself permission to do things that might not be immediately practical, or like you're not making time for things that are fun and beautiful in your life, it's got a lot to help you.

My main concern would be practical: if you follow the Artist's Way exercises to a T and do daily magical exercises, you mind find yourself pushing against the limits of what you can reasonably keep up with while also living your life. It's a common pattern I've run into myself with self-help type programs: you start off super excited, bite off more than you can chew, and when the initial enthusiasm wears off, something gives.

Hope that's helpful!

Sorry for the info dump, but I've struggled with this for years:

Personally, I had no contact with the Christian god while growing up in a pretty devout environment. Later, I realized that the conception of god in Christianity is so utterly confused (I'm talking on average and in general) that it is no wonder there isn't a consistent response. I think Greer is correct with his multiple-jesus theory. If you aren't picky about who shows up, it seem pretty easy to get an evil "Jesus" on the other end. That would also explain a lot of what I see in the local churches. They key for me was to realize that theology isn't a thing, its three: political crap that got shoe-horned in to solve disagreements, idle intellectual fantasies produced by a cultivated mind devoid of real spiritual contact, and organized accounts of spiritual experience. Trying to deal with god the father and work my way from there, I ended up in contact with a surprisingly pleasant entity calling itself YHVH who admitted to being a minor tribal deity who was later promoted into a role he isn't qualified for, serving as a representative of To En (The Unity in greek, I think this was a concession on his part to my neo-platonic reading). It may very well be that Christ was just a man, the divinity part came when he started being used as a mythological representation of either the solar logos or the higher self. When I tried to apologize for my earlier anger towards him (I can't speak for you, but I was a bit childish about it), his response was really striking: He would of course forgive me if I had actually offended him, but the truth of the matter is that he is in way over his head and isn't keeping up with his duties.

Regrading the old gods. I really like the "vague earthy form". Something I caught onto pretty quickly is that the impersonal powers of nature do still seem to have their own personality and temperament. During the SoP, I've had the elements start talking to me a few times.

When I started Druidry, I had no connection to, and little interest in, the welsh gods. To my surprise, Hu was very responsive to my prayer, and I am slowly getting to know the others. I think you are quite right that pick-and-choose isn't good, they are after all gods and not salad toppings. My best approach has been to ask about other deities and pantheons in prayer. Some I was warned against working with, others were completely fine, one I was told I'm simply not ready to work with yet. If you have a favorite (or at least, most interesting) you may wish to communicate with that deity, then work out into the rest of their pantheon and the other ones you are interested in. For historical reference, pantheons did get mixed historically. Isis was very popular far outside of Egypt, Mithras was originally persian and spread all through Rome, while a sizable minority of deities in Japan are imports that came with Buddhism. Hopefully this helps.

replies.title: Re: Magic Resistors and Covid

Former Buddhist here... This is a new low, even by the already abyssal standards of pop-buddhism, so I'm not sure where to begin.

To start, Tonglen is usually rendered as "giving & taking" which I regard as having much stronger interpersonal connotations. JMG is mostly correct. It is usually an advanced practice for laymen and an intermediate one for monastics, but not always. My teacher introduced me to it fairly early in the curriculum, but that is unusual. The normal safeguards for this practice are part of the surrounding philosophical context and not part of the practice itself. With that in mind, these instructions are shockingly bad.

Addressing Mollari in more detail:

Yeah, this practice would go spectacularly off the rails if somebody trained in western esotericism tried to do it. It is deeply grounded in eastern ideals of karma and merit, as well as the boddhisattva ideal. The verbal formula I learned was similar but noticeably different, "May I be free of suffering and it's causes. May I discover happiness (ananda, I believe) and it's causes." The next step wasn't "you" then "all beings", it was somebody you like, somebody you don't care about, then somebody you dislike/hate. The final stage (all beings) explicitly doesn't have a formula. The closing is done by allowing the light to fade and returning your attention to you breathing for a time. In mahayana (the larger school of thought that encompasses Tibetan Buddhism), it is believed that it is possible to exist in the world and be personally free from suffering as a Boddhisattva, but this assumes that you are actively working towards helping others attain liberation/insight.

I think there is a very good reason for having such a visceral response to it, though I don't think it will fuel the pandemic. The way the instructions are set up on the website, I believe they are guaranteed to simply draw in suffering, allow it to reverberate freely through their own life, and cut themselves off from the healing they so need. The instructions are very inward focused, especially in comparison to the original. The instruction page absolutely reeks to me, and I felt sick as soon as I opened it and immediately got better after closing it.

For more personal theories, I think this might be some of the very magical blowback that is being discussed. Remember that both the Dalai Lama and the Karmapa have both been accused of being fascists / white-supremacists / insert-insult-here. If some of their malignant-magic ended up targeting the Buddhist community, it is entirely possible that they ended up getting the attention of one of the Dharmapalas; and this might be a manifestation of its displeasure.

Regarding the vaccine as blowback, I really hope you are wrong, unfortunately I think the same thing. My attempts at scrying the vaccine have consistently been bad, and that theory seems to have a lot more metaphysical weight than the normal theorizing on the internet, for whatever that is worth.

comments.title: the flow of a creators energy into something she creates, and why completion may be important

This is an exploratory question.
When meditating on the three druid elements, I was pondering over how with intention and imagination we can impart energy into an object. For example, charging water. I recalled reading in one of Caroline Myss’ books about a native American tribe that believed that when a person creates something they put some of their own life energy into the object. It was the tribal belief that to start a creative undertaking while ill or elderly was a very dangerous thing that required permission of the tribes medicine man’s, or medicine woman’s, permission, because if the person died before the item they were crafting was completed, they would themselves be incomplete, their life force would be somrhow sundered, and their spirit might not not find its way.
It occurred to me that even if a person does complete a creative effort, it is true that some of their energy stays with that object: yet if the object is completed before death, this is not seen as a problem.

Thank you,
Gunnar

comments.title: Arthur Conan Doyle - Spiritualist after The Great War
comments.content:
I was wondering if you were going to write anything about Arthur Conan Doyle and his influence on the popularity of the spiritual movement in the aftermath of the Great War, or the effect of the Great War and Spanish 'flu on spiritualism and psychic communication.
One of my regular YouTube Channels (TimeGhost) -- produces a series "Between Two Wars Zeitgeist" (you can listen if you don't want to watch, since it's mostly still images anyway) -- just produced an episode for their second season on the rising prominence of spiritualism in the western world and the 1921 speaking tour by Doyle.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m6R_-zvCEBI&t=527s
Doyle dabbled in spiritualism before the war. After the horrific loss of life during the war and the 'flu afterwards, and the distraught families left behind, convinced him of the immense practical importance of psychic communication.
"By that point he also considered the proof to be irrefutable: a family friend, who had become a spiritualist after her three brothers were killed in the fighting at Ypres, was able to accurately relay to him a strictly personal conversation he once had with his wife's brother long before. Doyle could see no other explanation for how this friend could know this." Using his own Sherlock Holmes' famous dictum, "The Truth, however improbable, is what remains after the impossible is eliminated," Doyle the icon of rational science became Doyle the committed spiritualist and 100 years ago went on a speaking tour to promote the idea.
Even before 1914, he had been producing fewer works of fiction. Afterwards, he produced books entirely on spiritualism.
In 1926, he wrote "While it is true, that spiritualism counted its believers in millions before the war, there is no doubt that the subject was not understood by the world at large, and hardly recognized as having an existence. The war changed all that. The deaths occurring in almost every family in the land brought a sudden and concentrated interest in the life after death. People no only asked the question, "If a man dies shall he live again?" but they eagerly sought to know if communication was possible with the dear ones they had lost. They sought for the touch of a vanished hand, and the sound of a voice that is still."
Doyle said he was putting religion onto a scientific footing and that there was no contradiction between rational science and spiritual belief. That religion didn't need to fear or deny psychic communication and effects.
What do you suppose the influence of such a prominent household name -- even to this day -- had on the growth of spiritual movements at the time? His name has not yet come up in your writings, nor have you alluded to the change in public attitude that happened after those events, so I was wondering if you were going to explore that aspect, since I suspect many people who helped support the writers you have mentioned were attracted by exactly that zeitgeist.

Bruce
(Renaissance Man)

replies.title: Acting
replies.content:
I learned that Stanislavski "method acting" was a reaction against the Victorian mode of memorizing gestures and expressions for each emotion and building a role that way. i.e. loud noise [perform Surprise]. Lover enters through door [perform Delight] and so on. Obviously artificial, although it may have been effective for really talented actors it would have been a pathetic crutch for the untalented. As I was taught the "method" in college drama classes, one accessed one's own emotions and emotional memories in order to put together the role. Sure, you've never had a lover die in your arms, but what about a pet? And so on. But I don't recall (this was 50 years ago) being told to make the role as complete as you describe your Baal friend doing. Maybe that would have been more advanced work if I had been a drama major. Or maybe later generations expanded on Stanislavski's work in unhealthy directions, trying to get more bang for their buck. JMG has already written about how absorbing the adulation of an audience can distort the personality of a performer, which totally makes sense for live stage work. But we seem to see similar effects on those who only perform for the camera. I have jokingly remarked that the "primitive people" who supposedly feared that the white man's camera would take their soul were actually correct. It would explain a lot about actors, politicians, and in age of constant selfies--everyone. We just seem to be spreading ourselves too thin psychically.

BTW, sex columnist Dan Savage has advice for people who have worn a grove in their erotic imagination by focusing on only one technique or one fantasy and want to break out of it.

Rita

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Neopagan movement is a specific new religious movement which surfaced in Britain in the early 1950s, following Gerald Gardner's successful launch of his newly invented religion of Wicca, and spread to most of the English-speaking world thereafter. Not all Neopagans are Wiccans but the movement as a whole is heavily influenced by Wicca, especially in the forms that were popularized by pop-culture witches Starhawk and Margot Adler in the 1980s: faux-egalitarian (that is to say, groups are usually run by unelected covert hierarchies), affiliated with feminism and a range of left-wing political stances, and often distinctly unserious in their spiritual and religious commitments. Typical signals of membership are pentagrams, fantasy-fiction imagery (i.e., dragons, fairies, and these days, vampires), a faux-medieval ambience, and a fixation on "craft names" of the sort that Lady Pixie Moondrip parodied in detail.

As for why they hate ceremonial magic, I've always figured that it's too much work and risks getting real results. An edged joke I've heard more than once among ceremonial magicians suggests that Neopagans practice in the nude because that way, there's less laundry to wash if their magic actually works. (There are Neopagans of whom this is not true, but those I've met are comfortable with ceremonial magic and often practice it themselves.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you for the detailed and helpful definition.

replies.title: Neopagans and Ceremonial magic
replies.content:
To be fair--some, including myself, were put off by the Christian or Jewish imagery, god names and so forth in ceremonial magic. Others were put off by the personality of such prominent people as Alastair Crowley and the 101 people claiming to be the true head of the OTO or the Golden Dawn. Oberon Zell-Ravenheart, formerly Otter G'Zell, formerly Tim Zell, head of the Church of All Worlds, based on Heinlein's _Stranger in a Strange Land claims to have originated the term Neopagan to distinguish modern polytheists from classical and indigenous Pagans. CAW published a magazine "Green Egg" for many years which included a letters column in which many issued were raised and hashed out in print.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Neopagans and Ceremonial magic

Perhaps it is not possible to know in this day & age, but here goes: How common is it for people to get a 'notice' of someone who has just passed on (i.e., getting a whiff of a special fragrance, a dream, or a voice coming from 'nowhere', or a ball rolling for no reason, or a animal (or bird) stopping and gazing, etc.). This has not happened to me, but a family member and a few other people have mentioned these kind of events to me. (Me - I just get a feeling that's just to general to pin point until after the fact, or sometimes the feeling is just an unrelated depressive state. [or very rarely an image flashes through my mind (09/10/2001 - tower card from the Tarot, even though I hadn't seen a deck in decades. I recall, though, many people had some sort or premonition for that event - or maybe it was retrospective imagining].

Also, I know of a small child (age 3) whose father died in an accident not quite a year ago. She has seen his spirit on more than one occasion, and fairly recently. ("Look Mama! Daddy is here!"). They live far from where he passed on. How long before the child can no longer see or feel the presence of here father's spirit (which, by the way seems benevolent and reassuring). How often does this happen. The paternal grandmother has also felt his presence from time to time, so this perception might be familial.

replies.title: Re: Spirits of deceased
replies.content: These are far and away the most common form of ghostly experience; up to a third of people have such an experience at some time in their lives. As for the father, he may hang around for a while -- someone who dies suddenly while relatively young and strong will sometimes do so. As long as he's not being a problem to the living, and shows no signs of distress, it's probably okay.

comments.title: Last Week's Call for Links
comments.content:
A week ago a number of responders on this blog mentioned how many of their previously right leaning moderate friends were turning towards conspiracy theories. You asked for links you could use to support this. At first I could not find any, because people who don't trust the status quo are usually fairly quiet about it (especially when they are being censored on social media). And then I stumbled across this.

https://freerangeamerican.us/ammunition-shortage/

There are a number of articles just like it. Mainstream firearms publications produce documents saying something to the effect of, "here are all the logical reasons why we are suffering from the biggest ammo shortage in history... and no, its not because the government is secretly working with the ammo companies to buy up all your bullets."

Then the responders in the comments section say stuff to the effect of, "Yeah right, the free market gives us whatever we want. You are lying and working with the evil government."

This to me seems to represent a hard step to the right. We have something like 8.4 million new gun owners in this country since the beginning of 2020. Lots of people who used to feel safe are now saying, "I need to buy a gun." These are the same people who are saying they no longer trust their government or the news. I know several people who previously followed the progress meme and recently went full blown apocalypse meme. I think the fastest way to track this is through gun and ammunition sales, and to see to what extent gun owners can no longer even trust gun companies.

Anyway, I hope that helps you if you are looking to put a finger on the pulse of how the apocalypse meme is doing and how rapidly the mainstream is loosing control.

For whatever its worth, talk of open rebellion is also becoming more mainstream. Check out what a right wing website had to say about general Flynn and his ideas concerning when military coups are righteous and necessary. Long story short, killing our political leaders is not such a fringe conversation anymore...

replies.title: Re: Last Week's Call for Links
replies.content: Thanks for this. I saw Flynn's comment -- oog. We have a thing called the Constitution and, even when it doesn't produce the results somebody or other wants, it needs to be maintained; the alternatives are pretty grim.

comments.title: witchcraft
comments.content:
Archdruid,

Do you have any book recommendations for someone looking to get into witchcraft. My partner has strongly indicated that she indents to get into witchcraft practices and considering the amount of bad practices out there related to that egregor, I was curious if there's any material that's well structured.

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: witchcraft
replies.content: I'm sorry to say I don't -- I've never felt any attraction to that. Anybody else?

replies.title: Re: witchcraft
replies.content:
Syfen, If your wife is looking for Wicca as a religion I would recommend the Witches' Bible by Stuart and Janet Farrer. It will give a clear view of what the practice is like. Since the actual practice is secret or oathbound, the book is not exact, but the flavor is there. There are a number of types of British Traditional Witchcraft--Gardnarian, Alexandrian, Central Valley Wicca (Central Valley of California) and so on. Traditionalists will tell you that only BTW should be called Wicca, but the term has been adopted by so many groups that that ship has sailed. There are so many new books on the market that I can't keep up: finding a teacher takes patience and work and depends a lot on where you are located. New Wiccan Church as a website that may provide additional information--unfortunately Witchvox, which used to offer contact listings, has closed.
If she is looking for witchcraft as a magical practice, that is an entirely different matter. Some of the groups that emphasize magic call themselves 'traditional' witches and scorn any idea of "harm none". I don't follow them, so no advice.

Good luck, blessed be.

Rita

replies.title: Re: witchcraft
replies.content:
Aidan Wachter (Six Ways, Weaving Fate) would be an author to look into. I bought Six Ways to compliment the Celtic Golden Dawn with writing on offerings (outside of ritual), sigils, and non-ceremonial based pathworking. I highly recommend it. The book is thin, descriptive while concise, and builds a simple system with meditation, offerings, banishing, and divination. Aidan has a sparse but well-written website and seems to focus on working with spirits in a calm and fairly basic way. I'm also allergic to witchcraft, and this landed well. I'd go as far as this is the *only* witchcraft-like system out there I'd pick up material from. Spirits exist, meditation works, sigils get you TWS moments if they don't blow up in your face (TSW..), and simple practices adopted slowly can often improve your life.

I'm happily oriented towards years of meditations with the Qabalah through LRM and the Circles of Power curriculum, but this book stays on my shelf and encourages me to leave offerings for spirits and the ancestors now and then.

Aidan has a good interview or two on podcasts if she'd like an introduction.

Take care out there,

~ Wylie

comments.title: Reflections on and questions about initiation
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

As always, the gratitude I have for your time to help with planting and caring for the seeds of this community's desire for the occult pales with comparison to the light of a full moon.

I'll have to get to my questions by way of reflection on the birth of my son yesterday. It was a beautiful experience with much food for thought.

We decided to go the route of a home birth because of the mechanical feeling my wife was left with when visiting all the doctors. I'd probably have been more reticent about the choice but I've become open to thinking about things differently. Since doing a birth at home, I spent more time learning about the birthing process together with my wife, through reading and coaching from the midwife. One thing in particular that really impressed me was to learn that after the head crowns, the baby actually assists in pushing itself out with a "jumping" motion.

Our living room was turned into a sanctuary when the time came. Curtains were drawn, candles were lit, relaxing music was playing. A birthing pool served as an altar. The midwife and his assistant were cautious to let my wife and I lead through most of the process, but still were there to help orchestrate all of our energies together for the climactic moment.

Through it all, especially in reflecting over the moments, I kept seeing the initiation of initiations. A well developed ritual, ending in the initiate willingly passing through to the other side.

Perhaps these ideas weren't the source of the idea of initiations, a metaphor to use for other initiatory experiences, but I felt ultimately, it wasn't a wrong idea as to how they may have come about. 1) Was birth likely on of the influences of initiation?

With how so many modern births, in a cold, sterile environment, the woman forced upon a bed in uncomfortable positions, and the baby often torn from the womb, I can't help but think of the parallels to our mechanical society who believes in progress as the answer. 2) Would this play a part in, and feed into our belief of the cult of progress?

Lastly, I was reminded a lot of Plato's analogy of the cave. Within the womb, I learned that the child can see some light and shadow, along with able to hear. This is a lot like the people chained, watching the shadows and lights on the wall. When the baby is able to free itself, jumping out to the light, this is almost as if discovering the source of the shadows and lights on the wall. The final step will coming out of the cave in entirety. 3) Do you have any knowledge if the parallels I described were a source for Plato's cave analogy?

In conclusion, I know I have much to meditate upon. Thanks again though for taking your time.

Prizm

replies.title: Re: Reflections on and questions about initiation
replies.content:
Many, many congratulations! I'm glad to hear that it was a positive experience for all concerned; may your son thrive and accomplish wonderful things in this incarnation.

1) Yes, very much so. Many initiations -- including one that I've received -- explicitly draw on that imagery.

2) I don't know if it feeds into the cult of progress but it's certainly one of the more miserable manifestations of it. I have, at the very beginning of my memories, a dim recollection of feeling very cold and lonely and wretched, lying on my back unable to move, and staring up at a pale ceiling that was very far away. I have some reason to think that it's the last faint recollection of lying there in the maternity ward just after I was born. Let infants be with their mothers? What an unprogressive notion!

3) That's a fascinating speculation. I don't know that anyone's explored it, but it would be worth looking into.

replies.title: Re: Reflections on and questions about initiation
replies.content:
Especially since starting Levi's The Doctrine of High Magic, I've noticed a lot of life experiences are great rabbit holes to go down into and explore in ways I haven't. And this recent experience with the magic of ritual has shown me how powerful and meaningful ritual is.

I'm also now really looking forward to going over the next chapter :)

Thank you very much for your words of congratulations and well wishes.

Prizm

replies.title: Re: Reflections on and questions about initiation
replies.content:
A thought that just awakened to me with regards to initiation and birth parallels, just as the baby is hidden within the womb, the initiate to be is hidden from the worlds to be revealed, but once entered, while it may be overwhelming there allows the opportunity to also learn so much more than you could before entering.

I had been very hesitant to initiate before..

Prizm

comments.title: Intercepted Houses and a Grand Cross
comments.content:
Hello JMG, thank-you for the M&M's.. I mean MM's!

I've been untangling my natal chart for the past few months, and can hopefully ask a few coherent questions!

I've read your comment on grand crosses above - strong placements equals strong crosses. I'd like to ask you a follow-up question regarding grand crosses and intercepted houses.

My natal chart has a cardinal-sign grand cross with Venus (ruler of Ascendent) in Aries in the 12th house, opposing Jupiter retrograde in Libra in the 6th. Aries-Venus is intercepted by Pisces-ruled 12th, and Libra-Jupiter is intercepted by Virgo-ruled 6th. The cross is completed by Mars-fall-Cancer and Moon-detriment-Capricorn, with Moon conjunct Neptune, Uranus, and Midheaven.

1) Have you settled on a way of interpreting planets in intercepted signs, in natal or electional astrology? At a basic level, interception seems to deprive *planets* of influence in a chart. My Mars and Venus each rule one fewer house because Aries and Libra are intercepted, while my Moon and Saturn have more reach where Cancer and Capricorn rule two houses each.

2) Are oppositions and squares weakened (less held down) by intercepted planets and signs, or does interception mostly correlate with conflict between the ruler of a house and the planet and sign that is entering that space? For example, my 6th house is ruled by Virgo and Mercury, but retrograde Jupiter in Libra is sharing the same space, and is in apparent conflict with many of his neighbours.

I can answer part of this question already - I feel the cardinal aspect of this cross strongly! I was born 5.5 weeks premature, compulsively wake with first sunlight, and have learnt prioritization means refinement rather than working harder towards divergent outcomes. I identify with impulsive Aries Venus opposing ever-balancing Jupiter, inward-focused Mars, and outwardly driven Capricorn Moon.

I'm unsure if interceptions should be considered more in terms of their effect on the strength of raw planet-sign aspects, or if I should be looking for subtler ways intercepted houses may strengthen some planets and weaken others across the entire chart. My current read is to look for conflicts (learning opportunities, and potential sources of strength) between the sign and planetary ruler of a house, and the sign energy expressed by an intercepted planet within the house.

Thank you for your time!

~ Wylie

comments.title: The Wheel of Fortune - Justice
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I guess this goes a little afield, but figured I would get your take. Last week I had a series of misfortunes taking away my illusion of security and have ended up reflecting on my life all weekend as I wait to find out the damage. Uninspiring - the mean 3rd person summary is "entitled negative clown who keeps getting what they deserve". As I grew up, my mind became taken over by a split between my neurotic mother / strict religious schooling / fearfulness and "nice" and a sort of impulsive, selfish, power hungry rebellious side. I feel - I lost my soul, my ability to connect with my genuine feelings and care about my own interests or make good plans. Since then I've spent my life either trying to go into fields I associate with prestige and end up disapproving and dropping out or dropping out and obsessing about escaping my humiliations. I've never felt secure enough to have a real relationship. I veer between impulsiveness and negativity and increasingly am just stuck ruminating.

In my natal chart, I think this would be Saturn in Leo in the 10th house, which disposits my Sun in Capricorn in the 3rd. This constant obsession with the oppressive figure of The Mother and The Authorities and with wanting badly to make something of myself and be a success but somehow being too rebellious or perhaps misdirecting my efforts into situations that are negative. Also I'd guess that placement on my charts is responsible for periodically taking up various artist vocations, although I keep quitting due to feeling pressured to focus on "figure out what you are doing with your life". Also I feel in a weird sense my current stuck state of ruminating while Rome burns kind of IS a lifelong developing piece of edgy performance art, basically all last year's misfortunes have a hysterionic quality, failing and panicking is my creativity...

I feel stuck in a ruminating loop. I'm supposed to be doing my final project for a hypnotherapy class and feel I ought to do it so I'll have some kind of reasonable desperately needed income and position but feel obsessed by the social media hype side of the class and the idea that if I was more authentic, I would either focus on doing something meaningful, like focusing on my art or going back to school in acupuncture, and work in a factory or as a waitress maybe while trying to build up an astrology / tarot business. In short - my head is spinning and I feel even though I can see my history of rumination and bad choices, I'm unable to stop it, and will spend the rest of my life in too much financial stress and mental unhealth to enjoy love or really focus on anything.

So I wonder...

1) Should I just stop being so egotistic and focus on making money somehow so I can have a normal life, accept being "a loser"? It seems to me that Saturn in that sign and position is putting a demand on me to be "make something of myself" as a woman. I like that I've moved away from a city full of careerist self important people who only act "for ego" to a town where people seem more egalitarian and open to the flow of life, and had a big shrooms breakthrough about how the ego only exists to block out death and yet is disproved by it... but if I'm honest with myself, I realize that I'm just hiding from how disappointed I am in myself, how I want to earn respect.

2) I don't really know what to think of this karma. It just makes me depressed. My mother I'm sure didn't want to be a neurotic who messed up her kids. This stuff has eaten up my life... just had my Uranus return. I guess the point is I overcome it, I win for us all? But so far I go on thinking which perpetuates it.

comments.title: egregore vs architype
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

I listened to the most recent podcast you posted about the King in Orange. In it you make reference to the egregore, or a non person who nevertheless helps structure the thinking of a group of people. I am not sure how that differs from the Jungian architypes. Are these basically the same thing just using different vocabulary?

replies.title: Re: egregore vs architype
replies.content: No, they're different. An archetype is hardwired into the basic structure of your brain -- Jung says that the archetypes are how we experience our instincts. Egregors are created by groups of people concentrating on a common set of ideas and emotions -- they're not hardwired, they come into existence and can also go out of existence.
